lessonTitle: Unit 6: Section 3: Neurosis ................................................................................    18: understand abnormal behaviours from the context of knowing normal behaviour

targetOutcomes.title: understand abnormal behaviours from the context of knowing normal behaviour

prerequisiteKnowledge: categorize the impact of neurosis in terms of physical, mental, and emotional
misconceptions: Students may memorize terms without recognizing examples, counterexamples, or application contexts.
Students may produce chapter-note summaries instead of evidence-based explanations tied to the prompt.
Students may use Neurosis as a label without understanding how it connects to the assessed task.

sourceReferences.resourceTitle: ALBERTA DISTANCE LEARNING CENTRE SSN2171 Personal Psychology 20 KEY
sourceReferences.whySelected: Defines the exact performance demand students must meet.
sourceReferences.exampleSnippet:
Personal Psychology 20  Assignment Booklet 6

ADLC Personal Psychology 20  5

 _D__ 22.

sourceReferences.whySelected: Defines unit scope, outcome language, or required concepts.
sourceReferences.exampleSnippet:
Neurosis:  Mentally-Based Reactions
• Amnesia:  “forgetting”, which usually means personal memories and even self-
identity can be forgotten due to severe mental problems linked to emotions
The text also explains the fascinating phenomenon of fugue amnesia.
• Hypochondria:  falsely believing many illnesses are bodily present after reading
about or hearing about them
For example, someone sees a medical report on TV about brain cancer and is
convinced he or she has it.
• Obsessions:  constant, re-occurring thoughts that are irrational
• Compulsions:  actions that an individual seems compelled to do repeatedly, often
against the his or her will
• Tourette’s Syndrome:  a compulsive condition manifesting itself in the form of
motor tics (eye blinking, shoulder shrugging) or vocal tics (snorting, clearly the
throat)
Neurosis:  Emotionally-Based Reactions
• Phobias:  excessive, irrational fears
Phobias might arise from three situations:
◦   a trauma the individual personally experiences
◦   watching others react with fear and copying their behaviours
◦   misinterpreting data about a situation
Many interesting phobias are listed in the text, page 202.

whatThisLessonMustPrepareStudentsToDo: Provide, complete, write, apply, describe, explain in completed assignment booklet responses format for ALBERTA DISTANCE LEARNING CENTRE SSN2171 Personal Psychology 20 KEY.
checksForUnderstanding: Quick retrieval: define and use Neurosis, Abnormal, Anxiety in one accurate sentence.
Mini-explanation: explain Neurosis without reading notes.
Error check: identify a common misconception about Neurosis and correct it.
guidedPracticeIdeas: Model how to provide using the lesson vocabulary and one cited source example.
Walk through one assignment-booklet prompt from ALBERTA DISTANCE LEARNING CENTRE SSN2171 Personal Psychology 20 KEY and annotate what a successful response has to include.
Have students rehearse a response frame before they write independently.
independentPracticeIdeas: Complete a short practice task in the same assignment-booklet style used by ALBERTA DISTANCE LEARNING CENTRE SSN2171 Personal Psychology 20 KEY.
Use the required vocabulary list without copying a textbook paragraph.
Revise the response using the lesson's misconception and readiness checks.
evidenceOfReadinessForAssessment: Student can provide Neurosis accurately without reverting to chapter-note summary.
Student uses Neurosis, Abnormal, Anxiety in context.
Student completes a practice response that matches the success criteria language from ALBERTA DISTANCE LEARNING CENTRE SSN2171 Personal Psychology 20 KEY.
